title: Bradley-Terry Preference Loss
concept: The reward model is trained using Bradley-Terry loss: maximize the score difference between the chosen response and the rejected response.

exercises.prompt: What is the primary purpose of a Reward Model in AI training?

options.text: To score and rank AI responses based on quality
To translate text into different languages

exercises.explain: We train a reward model to evaluate how good or bad an AI's response is, helping guide the AI toward human-preferred behavior.

cards.title: Widening the Gap
cards.body: How do we teach an AI what humans actually prefer? We don't just tell it what's good—we show it two options and ask it to make the gap between the winner and the loser as wide as possible!

cards.title: What is Bradley-Terry Loss?
cards.body: The Bradley-Terry Preference Loss is a rule used to train Reward Models. Instead of giving absolute scores, it looks at pairs of responses (a chosen one and a rejected one) and trains the model to maximize the difference between their scores, making sure the preferred answer always wins.

cards.title: The Baking Contest
cards.body: Imagine you are a judge at a baking competition. Instead of giving every single cake a score out of 100, you are given two cakes at a time and asked: "Which one is better?" By choosing one over the other, you are implicitly ranking them. Bradley-Terry loss acts like this judge, guiding the AI to score the preferred cake higher than the rejected one.

cards.title: Comparing Two Poems
cards.body: If an AI is asked to write a poem, it might generate two options. Option A is beautiful (chosen) and Option B is gibberish (rejected). If the Reward Model scores Option A as +2.1 and Option B as -1.5, the Bradley-Terry loss is happy because there is a wide, positive gap favoring the chosen option!

cards.title: Absolute Scores Don't Matter
cards.body: People think a Reward Model must give perfect absolute scores, like a school grade. In reality, the absolute numbers don't matter! What matters is the relative difference. A score of 5 vs 2 is just as good as 105 vs 102, as long as the preferred option wins.

cards.title: The Score Balance
cards.body: See how the Bradley-Terry loss shifts the scores to favor the human preference.

left.text: Should receive a lower score from the reward model.

right.text: Should receive a higher score from the reward model.

exercises.prompt: What does the Bradley-Terry loss function compare during training?

options.text: Ten responses at the same time

exercises.explain: Bradley-Terry loss compares two responses at a time (a pairwise comparison) to figure out which one is better.

exercises.prompt: True or False: The Bradley-Terry loss wants the score of the chosen response to be much higher than the score of the rejected response.
exercises.explain: The goal of Bradley-Terry loss is to maximize the gap, meaning we want the chosen response to have a much higher score than the rejected one.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the missing word to complete how the Bradley-Terry loss works.
exercises.sentence: The Bradley-Terry loss trains the model by maximizing the score [blank] between the chosen and rejected response.
exercises.explain: Bradley-Terry loss focuses on widening the difference between the scores of the chosen and rejected options.

exercises.prompt: Which of the following describes an AI making a mistake during preference training?

options.text: A trainer choosing a polite response over a rude one.
A trainer choosing a correct math answer over an incorrect one.
An AI model choosing to give a higher score to a toxic response than a helpful one.

exercises.explain: Choice C represents an AI generated response because a real human trainer would choose the helpful response over the rude one.

exercises.prompt: How does the Bradley-Terry loss function help a Reward Model learn to rank AI responses?

options.text: It maximizes the difference between the chosen response score and the rejected response score.
It forces the AI to output a score of 100 for all good answers and 0 for bad ones.
It averages the scores of both responses to find a neutral middle ground.
It completely ignores the rejected response to focus only on the chosen one.

exercises.explain: Bradley-Terry loss focuses on the relative difference between two scores, not their absolute values. By maximizing the difference, the chosen response naturally gets a higher score than the rejected one.
